name: Cast into Flame
skills: Godot, Teamwork, Github version control, resolving complex merge conflicts
description: I made this game with Jack Sims, Rosalind Dixon, Phoebe Royer, Jackson McClame, and Carter Gruebel as our senior capstone project. We had 10 weeks to make it. I took the lead on programming and I was responsible for a lot of the tools and systems we used including, the character controller, enemy AI, mini-map, and world generation.

Player Controller.text: I made the player controller, using a state machine. My design for the player took inspiration from the game Core Keeper. The biggest feature we drew from Core Keeper was the player facing the direction of the mouse instead of which way they were going. This allowed for more advanced combat. The player was designed with animations for each action in each direction. One challenge I gave myself was allowing the player to change directions without resetting an animation. For example, if the player is in the middle of their stride, walking West and then turn North and continue walking, it will not reset the walking cycle. Unfortunately, we did not continue with the project, so we did not get finalized art to demonstrate the full functionality.

World Generation.text: I iterated on the world generations several times before settling on the final design. I took the opportunity prototype the tile-based world generation in a different class. I spent one week looking into Perlin-noise and other popular world generation techniques. You can find the prototype for the world generation here and the source code here. It wasn't made for users to actually see so pardon the buggy behavior when switching tabs. Before we reeled in the scope of the project, we planned to do 9 levels: one for each circle of Hell. So, I made used different generation techniques that would suit each level and experimented with different color palettes. For the project, we ended up using the generation technique made for the lust circle, which is a simple random walk generation. It made the most interesting layout that suited our gameplay best.

name: Tail of Two Tides
description: I made this game with Emily Tran, Michael Nieto and Garrett Blake for the GMTK's 2024 Game Jam. The theme was "Built to Scale", so we made a rhythm game about winning over a scaly mermaid. You alternate between navigating rocky waters and pressing the right key on note. I wrote the code for moving the pirate between lanes, instancing the obstacles, and giving the obstacles collision. I am particularly proud of how the pirate can change directions in the middle of moving from one lane to another.

Unity Coroutines.text: While working on this project, I learned how to use Unity coroutines. Coroutines are functions that take place over multiple game frames. In this case, when the player made an input, I would start a coroutine for moving the boat in the input direction (after checking that it would not move the boat out of bounds). Every frame, the coroutine would move the boat closer to its destination until it reached it, where it would terminate itself. I was particularly proud that the player could input in a different direction while the boat moved between lanes, and it would cancel the current coroutine and start a new one, moving in the newly inputted direction.
